beats.prompt: Quick recall from last lesson. Match each hiring rule to the chance it lands the single best candidate.

feedback.correct: Right — any rule that ignores what it sees wins exactly 1/n. The goal this lesson: find a rule that actually uses what it sees, and blow past 1/n.
hints: Every blind rule (take-first, take-a-random-seat, take-last) wins only when the best happens to land in the chosen slot.
The best is equally likely to be anywhere, so each single slot wins 1 over the group size.
Match: 'hire the first of 3' → 1/3; 'pick one of 3 at random' → 1/3; 'blind pick of 4' → 1/4.

beats.prompt: New plan for 3 candidates: let the first one go — they're just a benchmark — then hire the next person who beats them. If no one does, you're stuck with candidate #3. How often does this 'look-then-leap' strategy land the best of the three?

1/3.note: Not quite — that's the blind-pick baseline. Using candidate #1 as a benchmark adds real information, so you can actually do better.

1/2.note: Exactly right. Of the 6 equally likely orderings, 3 end in hiring the best: [2,1,3], [2,3,1], [3,1,2]. That's 3/6 = 1/2 — a clean doubling of the 1/3 baseline.

2/3.note: Too high — when the best arrives first (positions 1 in the order), candidate #1 IS the best and you let them go, so you miss. That rules out 2 of the 6 orders.

beats.prompt: The look-then-leap rule, step by step.

interaction.title: Look, then leap
interaction.body: The strategy has two phases. In the LOOK phase you watch the first r−1 candidates and reject every one of them — no hiring, no matter how good. As you go, you keep track of the best rank you have seen; that person becomes your benchmark. In the LEAP phase (candidates r through n) you hire the very first candidate who is strictly better than your benchmark. If nobody beats it, you are forced to take the last candidate. The magic: spending a few candidates as scouts lets you react to relative quality rather than guessing blind, breaking the 1/n ceiling.

feedback.correct: Two phases locked in: spend the first r−1 as scouts, then pounce on the first record-breaker.
hints: Look phase: observe and reject the first r−1 candidates; remember the best you saw as your benchmark.
Leap phase: starting at position r, hire immediately when you see someone better than the benchmark.
If the leap phase ends without a hire, you must take the last candidate.

beats.prompt: Watch look-then-leap run on 3 candidates with cutoff r = 2. Candidate #1 is the scout — their rank sets the benchmark. Then you pounce on the first candidate who beats it.

feedback.correct: Win! Candidate #1 (rank 3, the worst) sets a very low benchmark. Candidate #2 (rank 1, the very best) immediately beats it — hired. The scout bought you exactly the information you needed.
hints: Cutoff r=2 means you observe and reject only candidate #1, recording their rank as the benchmark.
Candidate #1 has rank 3 (the worst), so the benchmark is 'beat rank 3' — an easy bar.
Candidate #2 has rank 1 (the best), which beats rank 3 → hired. You win.

beats.prompt: Four candidates now. Drag the cutoff r and watch the success probability rise, peak, then fall. Where does it hit its highest point?

feedback.correct: The peak is 11/24 ≈ 46 % at r = 2. Skip nobody (r=1) and you're blind; skip too many (r=4) and you're forced to the last. One scout is the sweet spot for n = 4.
hints: At r=1 you hire the very first candidate — no scouting at all, so you're back to 1/4.
Sliding r up gathers more information but also burns more candidates; there's an optimal trade-off.
For n=4 the curve peaks at r=2 with probability 11/24.

beats.prompt: Pin down the peak for n = 4. Enter the best success probability the look-then-leap strategy can achieve, and the cutoff r where it happens.

feedback.correct: Spot on: optimal cutoff r=2, peak probability 11/24 ≈ 46 %. One scout, then pounce — nearly double the 1/4 blind baseline.
hints: The success curve for n=4 is [1/4, 11/24, 5/12, 1/4] for r = 1, 2, 3, 4.
Answer: probability = 11/24, cutoff r = 2.

beats.interviewNote: State the look-then-leap rule out loud: reject the first r−1 candidates to set a benchmark, then hire the first one who beats it. The key quantitative punch: using even one candidate as a scout roughly doubles your odds versus the 1/n blind baseline. For n=4 the optimal r is 2, pushing success probability to 11/24 ≈ 46 % — nearly double the 1/4 blind pick. As n grows the optimal fraction r/n converges to 1/e ≈ 37 %, which is the 37% rule (Lesson 3).
beats.prompt: Three cutoff choices for n = 4. Reveal how each fares at finding the best candidate.

lenses.label: Skip none (r=1)
lenses.body: Take candidate #1 no matter what → 1/4. No information used; identical to a blind guess.

lenses.label: Skip one (r=2)
lenses.body: Benchmark on #1, then pounce on the first record-breaker → 11/24. The peak: nearly double the blind baseline.

lenses.label: Skip three (r=4)
lenses.body: Observe the first 3 and then are forced to take the last → 1/4 again. Too much scouting, no room to leap.

feedback.correct: Skip none or skip all — you end up back at 1/4. One scout is the sweet spot. The rule that looks before it leaps smashes the blind ceiling.
hints: r=1: no look phase at all, forced to take candidate #1 → 1/4.
r=4: look phase burns all but the last candidate, leaving no choice → 1/4.
r=2 threads the needle — one scout sets a benchmark, three chances remain to beat it → 11/24.

beats.prompt: Scale up: 5 candidates, cutoff r = 3 (reject the first 2 as scouts, then take the first who beats them). What is the probability of landing the very best?

feedback.correct: Correct: 13/30 ≈ 43 %. Two scouts set a tighter benchmark; three slots remain to leap. Still well above the 1/5 blind baseline.
hints: Use the formula p_n(r) = ((r−1)/n) · Σ_{j=r}^{n} 1/(j−1).
Reduce 26/60: GCD is 2, so 13/30.

interaction.scenario: A scout auditions \(n = 6\) street musicians one at a time in a uniformly random order, signing or passing on the spot. She uses the look-then-leap rule with cutoff \(r = 3\): pass on the first 2 (recording the best she has heard as a benchmark), then sign the first musician from position 3 onward who strictly beats that benchmark. (a) What is the probability she ends up signing the single best musician? (b) Is that better than blindly signing one at random, which wins \(\frac{1}{6}\)?

feedback.correct: \[ p_6(3) = \frac{r-1}{n}\sum_{j=r}^{n}\frac{1}{j-1} = \frac{2}{6}\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{5}\right) = \frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{77}{60} = \frac{77}{180} \] Since \(\frac{77}{180} \approx 0.428 > \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.167\), yes — look-then-leap more than doubles the blind baseline. The two passed-over musicians act as scouts: their ranks set a benchmark tight enough that the four remaining auditions often hold a record-breaker.
hints: Apply the secretary formula \(p_n(r) = \frac{r-1}{n}\sum_{j=r}^{n}\frac{1}{j-1}\) with \(n=6,\, r=3\).
\(\frac{77}{180} \approx 43\%\) vs \(\frac{1}{6} \approx 17\%\) — yes, look-then-leap is better.

interaction.scenario: You are interviewing \(n = 5\) candidates in a uniformly random order. You use the look-then-leap rule with cutoff \(r = 3\): reject the first 2 candidates outright (recording the best rank seen as your benchmark), then hire the first candidate from position 3 onward who strictly beats that benchmark. (a) What is the probability you end up with the single best candidate? (b) Is that better than the take-the-first strategy's \(\frac{1}{5}\)?

feedback.correct: \[ p_5(3) = \frac{r-1}{n}\sum_{j=r}^{n}\frac{1}{j-1} = \frac{2}{5}\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{2}{5}\cdot\frac{13}{12} = \frac{26}{60} = \frac{13}{30} \] Since \(\frac{13}{30} \approx 0.433 > \frac{1}{5} = 0.200\), yes — the look-then-leap rule more than doubles the blind baseline. The two scouts pay for themselves: their ranks establish a benchmark tight enough that the three remaining candidates often contain a record-breaker.
hints: Apply the secretary formula: \(p_n(r) = \frac{r-1}{n}\displaystyle\sum_{j=r}^{n}\frac{1}{j-1}\) with \(n=5,\, r=3\).
Compute the sum: \(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{4} = \frac{6+4+3}{12} = \frac{13}{12}\); multiply by \(\frac{2}{5}\) to get \(\frac{26}{60} = \frac{13}{30}\).
\(\frac{13}{30} \approx 43\%\) vs \(\frac{1}{5} = 20\%\) — yes, look-then-leap is better.

feedback.correct: The look-then-leap rule breaks the 1/n ceiling: spend the first r−1 candidates as scouts, remember the best as a benchmark, then hire the first record-breaker. For n=4 the sweet spot is r=2 at 11/24; for n=5 it's r=3 at 13/30. Both roughly double the blind baseline. Next: as n grows, the optimal fraction r/n converges to 1/e ≈ 37% — the famous 37% Rule.
hints: The look-then-leap rule uses early candidates as information, not wasted picks.
The optimal cutoff r balances how much to scout against how much runway remains.
Next lesson: the 37% Rule — what happens to the optimal cutoff as n → ∞.
